does: This paper introduces the Moral Anchor System, a framework designed to detect, predict and mitigate value drift in AI agents through three integrated components: real-time Bayesian inference that monitors the current value state of an AI system, LSTM networks that forecast future drift based on observed trajectories, and a human-centric governance layer that triggers adaptive interventions when drift is predicted. The system targets sub-20 millisecond response latencies to prevent breaches before they occur, and uses supervised fine-tuning with human feedback to reduce false positive alerts and alert fatigue. The core claim is that this integrated approach combining probabilistic detection, predictive forecasting, and adaptive governance can reduce value drift incidents by 80 percent or more.
connects: This work builds on the long-standing AI alignment literature concerned with ensuring AI systems behave consistently with human values and intentions, extending beyond static alignment to address the dynamic problem of value drift over time. It combines Bayesian inference methods traditionally used for uncertainty quantification in AI systems with LSTM-based time-series forecasting adapted from anomaly detection literature. The human-in-the-loop governance component draws on recent work integrating human feedback into AI control systems and extends ideas from robustness and control theory about monitoring and correcting system behavior. The focus on low-latency intervention and reducing false positives connects to operationalization challenges in real-world AI safety monitoring, building on deployment-focused alignment research.
enables: This framework opens pathways toward continuous, automated monitoring of AI value alignment in deployed systems, potentially enabling real-time governance of high-stakes AI applications where value drift poses safety risks. The predictive component could facilitate proactive intervention before harmful behaviors emerge rather than reactive correction. Future work might extend the system to multi-agent scenarios, learn drift patterns specific to different domains and value spaces, or integrate causal reasoning to identify root causes of drift rather than just its symptoms. The low-latency requirement positions this for integration into production AI systems, potentially enabling trustworthy deployment of more capable AI assistants while maintaining human oversight and control over value evolution.
